participants.extracted: The trial enrolled 298 undergraduate and graduate students from American University and surrounding colleges (including Georgetown and the University of the District of Columbia). Mean age was 25.5 years; 40% were men. The sample was racially diverse, including 62% Caucasian, 16% African American, 7% Asian American, 4% Hispanic, and 11% other/undisclosed. Eligibility required current enrollment through August 2006. Students were excluded for a history of hypertension, hypoglycemia, chronic fainting, coronary heart disease, or current blood pressure above 140/90 mm Hg or below 90/60 mm Hg. A high-risk subgroup was defined by one or more hypertension risk factors: systolic blood pressure >130 mm Hg, diastolic blood pressure >85 mm Hg, family history of hypertension, overweight, or obesity (BMI ≥25).

interventions.extracted: Participants randomized to the intervention arm received instruction in the Transcendental Meditation (TM) technique, a standardized mind-body practice taught by certified instructors. The TM program consisted of a seven-step course: a group introductory lecture on potential benefits and prior research, a group preparatory lecture on the mechanics and origin of the technique, a personal interview, a personal instruction session, and three consecutive days of group verification and validation sessions to confirm correct practice. After training, participants were instructed to practice TM twice daily for 20 minutes while sitting comfortably with eyes closed, and they were invited to attend follow-up meetings with the instructor weekly for the first month and then monthly thereafter to support regular practice. TM instruction was delivered immediately after randomization; control participants were placed on a wait-list/delayed-start condition and received TM instruction after post-test assessment.

outcomes.extracted: The primary outcome was casual blood pressure, measured as systolic blood pressure and diastolic blood pressure in a seated position using an automated BP monitor (A&D Medical UA-787) with three readings taken at baseline and again at post-test after approximately 3 months; the last two readings were averaged for the study score. Secondary outcomes were psychological distress and coping ability, assessed at the same baseline and approximately 3-month post-test time points. Psychological distress was measured using the Profile of Mood States (POMS), including the total mood disturbance main scale and the tension/anxiety, depression/dejection, and anger/hostility subscales. Coping ability was measured using the Constructive Thinking Inventory global constructive thinking main scale.
